fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the schema annotation tool we've been building. The core idea is that developers waste a ton of time manually keeping database schema comments up to date in their model files — they go stale the moment someone runs a migration and nobody notices until something breaks in code review. We want a CLI tool that can read a table description and spit out a nicely formatted comment block, then insert or remove it from source files automatically.

A few things I want to make sure we nail: the comment blocks should support a few different documentation styles — I remember we talked about at least three or four of these in the last planning session, including that one Markdown style and the two Ruby doc formats we use internally. Also the route file annotation side of things needs to work too, not just the schema side.

One thing I'm fuzzy on is the exact alignment logic — like how wide should the name column be versus the type column, and how does that change when someone adds column comments? Also I keep hearing about the 'auto-generated foreign key name abbreviation' behavior, can someone confirm exactly how that works? And what happens when someone passes --pc before -p in the CLI args vs after? That ordering behavior tripped us up in the login module refactor last quarter. The file structure should definitely not be a single giant file.

Quick follow-up after the questions that came in: the alignment piece is per table, not some global fixed thing. The name column width is whatever the longest displayed column name is for that table, and that displayed string includes the comment suffix when comments are turned on, like '(full name)', then we add one extra space of padding. The type column is always padded to 18 characters. So both widths are based on the actual columns on that table, with only the type side using that fixed 18 value.

On the foreign key naming bit, we should abbreviate to 'fk_rails_...' only when the name matches the Rails auto-generated shape: it starts with 'fk_rails_' and then has exactly 10 hexadecimal characters after that. In that case we replace just that generated suffix with '...'. If 'show_complete_foreign_keys' is true, we show the full name instead. If the foreign key has no name at all, we fall back to the column name.

Also confirming the CLI ordering behavior: the per-file-type placement flags like --pc and the global -p / --position are parsed in argument order. If somebody explicitly sets a per-file-type value first and then later passes the global flag, the global flag only fills in the per-file-type settings that have not been explicitly set yet, and it does not clobber the one they already chose. So '--pc top -p bottom' ends up with position_in_class=top and all others=bottom. Same rule in the other direction too: explicit per-file-type wins for that file type, and the global value is really just the default for anything still unset.

And on rendering modes, we do support four total styles. There’s the default hash-comment block style with normal aligned Ruby comments, then Markdown via 'format_markdown': true using Markdown headings, a backtick table name, and a pipe-delimited column table, then RDoc via 'format_rdoc': true using definition-list style markup with bold names and inline-code type strings, and YARD via 'format_yard': true using @!attribute lines plus @return tags that map DB types to Ruby class names. In YARD mode the mappings are integer -> Integer, string/text/citext -> String, datetime/timestamp/time -> Time, date -> Date, boolean -> Boolean, float -> Float. Anything without a clean mapping, like decimal/numeric, json, jsonb, hstore, etc., should use [] in the @return tag, and we should leave off length/precision suffixes entirely there.

trigger_keywords: how does multiline check constraint expression get rendered
newlines in check constraint expressions
collapsing multi-line SQL expressions
check constraint expression whitespace normalization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Multi-line check constraint expressions are collapsed into a single line: all newline characters are replaced by spaces, sequences of multiple spaces are collapsed into a single space, and any trailing whitespace is trimmed. So a CASE expression spanning multiple lines becomes a single space-separated inline string. A constraint whose expression is null shows only its name with no parentheses.

trigger_keywords: route annotation file path
what file does the routes command target
default routes file location
where does the route map get inserted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The route annotation targets 'config/routes.rb' as the default file path. This path is always reported in the output as 'file=config/routes.rb'. When the file is not present (file_present=false), the tool reports 'status=not_found' and stops. When inserting, by default the annotation block is appended after existing content separated by a blank line; a 'top' position option prepends it instead.

trigger_keywords: how should the project be structured
directory layout for the source files
what goes in src vs tests
file organization requirements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-file directory structure with at minimum a 'src/' directory for core domain logic (column formatter, type formatter, default formatter, section builders for indexes/foreign-keys/check-constraints, style renderers, argument parser, file rewriting) and a 'tests/' directory for the test suite. A single-file monolith is explicitly prohibited. Each distinct responsibility (type formatting, default formatting, each section builder, each style renderer, CLI argument parsing, file rewriting) must be a separate module or class.

trigger_keywords: what does the -e flag do
exclude option comma list behavior
how are excluded groups parsed
exclude_tests and exclude_fixtures flags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The -e / --exclude flag accepts a comma-separated list of artifact group names. Each named group in the list enables a corresponding boolean setting: 'tests' sets exclude_tests=true, 'fixtures' sets exclude_fixtures=true, and similarly for other supported group names. The groups are expanded individually, so '-e tests,fixtures' yields both exclude_tests=true and exclude_fixtures=true as separate output settings.

trigger_keywords: how does the route removal work
stripping the route annotation block
what gets preserved when removing route comments
trailing blank lines after route annotation removal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Route annotation removal strips the entire block starting from the '# == Route Map' marker line through all following hash-comment lines. Blank lines that were introduced between the file content and the annotation block are also trimmed from the side adjacent to the annotation (trailing blank lines trimmed when annotation was at end, leading blank lines trimmed when annotation was at the top). Genuine file content and unrelated comment lines before the annotation are preserved intact. The status reported is 'status=removed'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'schema', 'cli', 'add', 'routes', 'remove'. Each value maps to one feature.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Here are the public test cases for feature3_2_route_map_remove:
{
  "description": "Removing a route-map annotation block from a routes file. The titled, hash-commented annotation block is stripped while genuine file content and unrelated leading comment lines are preserved. When the annotation was trailing, trailing blank lines introduced by it are trimmed but leading blank lines are left intact; when the annotation was leading, the reverse holds. The reported status indicates that annotations were removed.",
  "cases": [
    {
      "input": {
        "op": "routes",
        "action": "remove",
        "file_present": true,
        "routes_file_content": "


Rails.application.routes.draw do
  root 'root#index'
end


# == Route Map
#
# another good line
# good line
"
      },
      "expected_output": "status=removed
file=config/routes.rb



Rails.application.routes.draw do
  root 'root#index'
end
"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: the auto-generated foreign key name abbreviation behavior
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fk_rails_... abbreviation logic in feature1_5_foreign_keys.json: names matching the pattern /^fk_rails_[0-9a-f]{10}$/ are displayed as 'fk_rails_...' unless the show_complete_foreign_keys option is true.

context_pointers.vague_reference: how wide should the name column be versus the type column
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Dynamic name-column width = max display name length + 1; type column padded to fixed 18 chars. The display name includes the '(comment)' suffix when with_column_comments is enabled, as demonstrated in feature1_7_column_comments.json.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that exact behavior documented in my notes — you'll want to check the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the authoritative expected inputs and outputs, or look at the PRD sections in start.md that correspond to the feature number. Each feature has its own test file that acts as the ground-truth spec.

feature1_10_rdoc_format.json.description: RDoc rendering mode. Each column renders as an RDoc definition entry pairing the column name with its type and attributes in inline-code markup, and the block is wrapped with the RDoc begin/end schema markers.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: users
#
# *id*::   <tt>integer, not null, primary key</tt>
# *name*:: <tt>string(50), not null</tt>
#--
# == Schema Information End
#++

feature1_11_yard_format.json.description: YARD rendering mode. Each column renders as a YARD attribute directive followed by a return-type line, where the database type is mapped to a corresponding language class name and length suffixes are omitted.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: users
#
# @!attribute id
#   @return [Integer]
# @!attribute name
#   @return [String]
# @!attribute balance
#   @return []
# @!attribute created_at
#   @return [Time]
#

feature1_1_basic_column_block.json.description: Rendering the core annotation block for a table: an opening header line, a blank comment line, the table-name line, one formatted line per column listing its name, its type and any non-null marker, and a closing blank comment line. A column flagged as the table primary key is marked accordingly, and a column that permits null omits the non-null marker.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: users
#
#  id   :integer          not null, primary key
#  name :string(50)       not null
#

feature1_2_type_formatting.json.description: Formatting of the column type token. String-like types append their length in parentheses, decimal types render their precision and scale, a type whose length is supplied as a list renders that list as a separate attribute while keeping a bare type token, list-backed columns gain an array marker, and the set of types whose length is suppressed can be overridden through an option.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: products
#
#  id    :integer          not null, primary key
#  title :string(120)      not null
#  price :decimal(10, 2)   not null
#

feature1_3_default_values.json.description: Display of column default values as an attribute. Numeric defaults are shown verbatim, boolean defaults are shown as upper-case keywords, string defaults are shown quoted, a column without a default omits the attribute entirely, and types configured to suppress defaults never show one.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: accounts
#
#  id      :integer          not null, primary key
#  active  :boolean          default(TRUE), not null
#  balance :integer          default(0), not null
#  tier    :string(20)       default("guest"), not null
#

feature1_4_indexes.json.description: An indexes section appended after the columns when index display is enabled. Each index renders its name and parenthesised column list, with markers for uniqueness, a partial predicate, a non-default access method, and per-column ordering. A separate inline mode instead annotates each indexed column on its own line.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: users
#
#  id    :integer          not null, primary key
#  email :string(255)
#
# Indexes
#
#  index_users_on_email          (email) UNIQUE
#  index_users_on_team_and_role  (team,role)
#

feature1_5_foreign_keys.json.description: A foreign keys section appended after the columns when foreign-key display is enabled. Each entry shows its name and the referencing-to-referenced column mapping, optionally with cascade actions. Auto-generated names are abbreviated unless full names are requested, an unnamed key falls back to its column, and a custom target column is honoured.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: orders
#
#  id      :integer          not null, primary key
#  user_id :integer
#
# Foreign Keys
#
#  fk_rails_...  (user_id => users.id)
#

feature1_6_check_constraints.json.description: A check-constraints section appended after the columns when check-constraint display is enabled. Each constraint renders its name followed by its boolean expression in parentheses; a constraint with no expression shows only its name; multi-line expressions are collapsed to a single spaced line; entries are sorted by name and the name column is width-aligned. A Markdown rendering mode emits the same information as a bulleted list with code-formatted names and expressions.

check_constraints.name: multiline_test
check_constraints.expression:
CASE
  WHEN (age >= 18) THEN (age <= 21)
  ELSE true
END

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: users
#
#  id  :integer          not null, primary key
#  age :integer
#
# Check Constraints
#
#  alive               (age < 150)
#  missing_expression
#  multiline_test      (CASE WHEN (age >= 18) THEN (age <= 21) ELSE true END)
#  must_be_adult       (age >= 18)
#

feature1_7_column_comments.json.description: Column comment rendering. When comment display is enabled, each column whose comment is present appends the comment text in parentheses after its name, and the appended text participates in the alignment width of the block.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: users
#
#  id(identifier)  :integer          not null, primary key
#  name(full name) :string(50)       not null
#

feature1_8_ordering_and_filters.json.description: Column ordering and filtering controls. Columns may be sorted alphabetically, or grouped by a classification that places the primary key first followed by ordinary, timestamp and association columns. Columns matching an ignore pattern are dropped, and an optional schema version line may be appended to the header.

cases.expected_output:
# == Schema Information
#
# Table name: items
#
#  alpha :string(5)        not null
#  id    :integer          not null, primary key
#  zeta  :string(5)        not null
#

feature1_9_markdown_format.json.description: Markdown rendering mode. The header and section titles switch to Markdown headings, the table name is wrapped in code formatting, and columns render as a Markdown table with aligned name, type and attribute cells. Index and foreign-key sections render as Markdown lists.

cases.expected_output:
# ## Schema Information
#
# Table name: `users`
#
# ### Columns
#
# Name         | Type               | Attributes
# ------------ | ------------------ | ---------------------------
# **`id`**     | `integer`          | `not null, primary key`
# **`email`**  | `string(255)`      | `not null`
#

feature2_1_command_selection.json.description: Resolving the leading sub-command word of an argument vector into a selected command identifier. The recognised words select the model-annotation command, the route-annotation command, the help command, and the version command respectively. When no recognised command word is present, the help command is selected by default. The default action remains the annotate action unless changed by other arguments.

cases.expected_output:
command=models
target_action=do_annotations

command=routes
target_action=do_annotations

command=help
target_action=do_annotations

feature2_2_annotation_positioning.json.description: Parsing of annotation-placement arguments. A global placement argument accepts one of the keywords before, top, after, or bottom and applies it to every per-file-type placement setting simultaneously. Per-file-type placement arguments set only their own target. When a per-file-type placement is given an explicit value, a subsequent global placement does not override that already-set per-file-type value.

cases.expected_output:
command=help
position=before
position_in_class=before
position_in_factory=before
position_in_fixture=before
position_in_routes=before
position_in_serializer=before
position_in_test=before
target_action=do_annotations

command=help
position=bottom
position_in_class=top
position_in_factory=bottom
position_in_fixture=bottom
position_in_routes=bottom
position_in_serializer=bottom
position_in_test=bottom
target_action=do_annotations

feature2_3_flags_and_values.json.description: Parsing of boolean switches and value-bearing options into normalized settings. Switches turn a named setting on (delete-mode flips the action to removal; index display, foreign-key display, and check-constraint display each enable their section). One composite switch enables two related settings at once. A comma list option for excluded artifact groups expands into one enabled setting per named group. Value options capture their argument verbatim (a column-ignore pattern, a hide-limit type list). A repeatable path option appends to any previously supplied value rather than replacing it.

cases.expected_output:
command=help
target_action=remove_annotations

command=help
exclude_fixtures=true
exclude_tests=true
target_action=do_annotations

command=help
require=some_dir/,another_dir
target_action=do_annotations

feature3_1_route_map_add.json.description: Inserting a route-map annotation block into a routes file. Given the current file content and the raw route listing (prefix, verb, URI pattern, controller#action per line), the tool produces an updated file containing a titled, hash-commented route map. Each route line is preserved and right-aligned under a header row. By default the annotation is appended after existing content separated by a blank line; a top placement option instead prepends it. A Markdown mode renders the routes as a pipe-delimited table. The reported status indicates whether the file was changed.

input.rails_routes_output:
Prefix Verb       URI Pattern                  Controller#Action
             myaction1 GET        /url1(.:format)              mycontroller1#action
             myaction2 POST       /url2(.:format)              mycontroller2#action

cases.expected_output:
status=annotated
file=config/routes.rb

# == Route Map
#
#                 Prefix Verb       URI Pattern                  Controller#Action
#              myaction1 GET        /url1(.:format)              mycontroller1#action
#              myaction2 POST       /url2(.:format)              mycontroller2#action
status=not_found

feature3_2_route_map_remove.json.description: Removing a route-map annotation block from a routes file. The titled, hash-commented annotation block is stripped while genuine file content and unrelated leading comment lines are preserved. When the annotation was trailing, trailing blank lines introduced by it are trimmed but leading blank lines are left intact; when the annotation was leading, the reverse holds. The reported status indicates that annotations were removed.

input.routes_file_content:
Rails.application.routes.draw do
  root 'root#index'
end


# == Route Map
#
# another good line
# good line

cases.expected_output:
status=removed
file=config/routes.rb



Rails.application.routes.draw do
  root 'root#index'
end
